comment_text.sn36.24:1.1: In this discourse, the Buddha recalls his reflection on feeling as part of his path to awakening. In the next, he describes the awakening itself.

root_text.sn36.24:1.1: “Pubbeva me, bhikkhave, sambodhā anabhisambuddhassa bodhisattasseva sato etadahosi:
root_text.sn36.24:1.2: ‘katamā nu kho vedanā, katamo vedanāsamudayo, katamā vedanāsamudayagāminī paṭipadā, katamo vedanānirodho, katamā vedanānirodhagāminī paṭipadā?
root_text.sn36.24:1.3: Ko vedanāya assādo, ko ādīnavo, kiṁ nissaraṇan’ti?
root_text.sn36.24:1.6: sukhā vedanā, dukkhā vedanā, adukkhamasukhā vedanā.
root_text.sn36.24:1.10: yo vedanāya chandarāgavinayo chandarāgappahānaṁ. Idaṁ vedanāya nissaraṇan’”ti.

translation_text.sn36.24:0.2: 3. The Explanation of the Hundred and Eight
translation_text.sn36.24:1.1: “Mendicants, before my awakening—when I was still unawakened but intent on awakening—I thought:
translation_text.sn36.24:1.2: ‘What is feeling? What’s the origin of feeling? What’s the practice that leads to the origin of feeling? What’s the cessation of feeling? What’s the practice that leads to the cessation of feeling?
translation_text.sn36.24:1.3: And what is feeling’s gratification, drawback, and escape?’
translation_text.sn36.24:1.9: Craving is the practice that leads to the origin of feeling …
translation_text.sn36.24:1.10: Removing and giving up desire and greed for feeling: this is its escape.’”
